title: Backstrap loom warping basics
subtitle: Winding a continuous warp between two end sticks for plain weave on a backstrap loom
excerpt: A backstrap loom warps as a continuous loop wound around two end sticks: one tied to a fixed point, the other held to the weaver's body by a strap around the lower back. Tension is controlled by leaning forward and back. This guide covers measuring, winding, and setting up a plain-weave warp in 8/2 cotton at 16 epi.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from Andean and Mesoamerican weaving tradition. The continuous-warp method is documented in V&A textile collection records and across multiple cataloguing institutions; Andean backstrap weaving is described in Cason and Cahlander, The Art of Bolivian Highland Weaving (1976), used here for structural substance only. No quoted text.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wide band of cloth, leather, or cord that passes around the weaver's lower back and attaches to the breast beam of a backstrap loom. The weaver controls warp tension by leaning away from the fixed tie point; leaning forward releases tension, leaning back increases it.

A warping method in which a single length of yarn winds back and forth around two end sticks to form a loop rather than being cut into individual warp ends. The warp is not cut until the piece is finished; the loop structure gives the loom its portability and allows the weaver to adjust length without re-dressing.

content.text: A backstrap loom is a tensioned-warp loom with no fixed frame. One end beam ties to a wall, post, or tree; the other attaches to a strap worn around the weaver's lower back. Warp tension comes from the weaver's body weight rather than from a mechanical beam. The loom packs flat when removed from the tie point and folds into a bag. Warping uses a

: a single strand of 8/2 cotton wound back and forth around two end sticks to form a closed loop, rather than a set of cut individual ends. The shed is created by a shed rod and a heddle of string loops. Plain weave suits the first warp because the threading is a simple alternating shed: the shed rod lifts every other thread, the heddle pulls down the rest.

items.name: 8/2 cotton yarn (warp), cone
items.qty: Enough for the planned warp length plus 30 cm extra per end

items.name: 8/2 cotton yarn (weft), same or contrasting colour
Two round end sticks (dowels), 30 cm long, smooth
Shed rod, flat and narrow, same width as planned weaving
String heddle cord, smooth, firm twist, 1 mm diameter
Backstrap strap, 5-8 cm wide, 80 cm long
Fixed tie point: a door handle, post clamp, or wall hook at hip height
Warping board (optional, for measuring consistent warp length)
Blunt darning needle (for weaving in warp tails)

content.text: At 16 epi (ends per inch), a 20 cm (8 in) wide cloth needs 128 warp ends. In a continuous warp, each pass around the two sticks counts as two ends. You therefore need 64 passes. Plan the warp length as: finished weaving length + 15 cm take-up + 30 cm loom waste at each end. For a 40 cm sample, wind to 100 cm measured around the circuit.

Fix the two end sticks parallel, spaced the planned warp circuit length apart. Tie the yarn to one stick with a slip knot at one edge. Wind the yarn around the far stick, back around the near stick, and repeat, keeping even tension on each pass. Count each full circuit as two ends. Spread the passes evenly across the sticks as you go; do not allow them to drift to the centre. After 64 passes, cut and tie off at the starting stick. The warp now forms a flat loop with 128 ends across the two sticks.

Slide the shed rod through the warp, picking up every other end. The rod creates the natural shed: threads over the rod form one shed layer, threads under the rod form the other. Work from one selvedge to the other, checking that the count alternates correctly. Secure the rod with cord loops at each end so it cannot slide out.

The string heddle lifts the threads the shed rod does not hold. Cut 64 loops of heddle cord, each 20 cm long. Pass each loop around one thread that lies under the shed rod and knot it to a heddle stick held above the warp. When the heddle stick is lifted, it pulls those threads above the shed rod, creating the counter-shed. Work across the full warp width before testing the shed.

around the breast beam (the stick nearest the weaver) and tie the other end of the loom to the fixed wall hook or door handle at hip height. Sit on the floor or on a low seat with both legs forward. Lean back gently until the warp is taut. Lean forward to release tension. The warp should feel firm without being rigid: threads should not vibrate like a guitar string, but should resist a sideways push with moderate force.
Step 6: Open the sheds and weave the header

Test the sheds before winding on any weft. Slide the shed rod on edge to open the first shed, then lift the heddle stick to open the counter-shed. Both sheds should open cleanly with no tangled threads. Weave four to six passes of scrap yarn as a header, beating firmly with the fingers or a flat stick. The header spreads the warp ends to the correct spacing and reveals any threading errors before weaving the project yarn.

items.problem: The warp ends are bunched in the centre and spread thin at the edges
items.cause: Passes drifted to the centre of the sticks during winding
items.fix: Unwind the warp and re-wind, actively guiding each pass to the correct position across the sticks as you go. Use small pencil marks on the sticks to mark the selvedge positions before starting.

items.problem: The shed rod drops one end count on every other shed
items.cause: The pick-up pattern shifted by one thread at some point during rod insertion
items.fix: Remove the rod, check the count from the left selvedge, and re-insert, confirming the alternating over-under sequence at every fifth end. A threading error at one point propagates across the rest of the warp.

items.problem: The warp goes slack or pulls the wall hook loose during weaving
items.cause: The tie point is not fixed firmly, or the backstrap slips up over the hips
items.fix: Use a wall hook rated for at least 5 kg, or tie to a door handle. Wear the backstrap low, at the widest part of the hips rather than at the waist, and check the position after the first few picks.
